King Lemuel's mother confronts her son with an urgent appeal that cuts straight to the heart of power and its corruptions. She does not flatter him with his status. Instead, she warns him away from the very things that destroy rulers—sexual indulgence and drunkenness—because these vices do not merely weaken a king's character; they silence his voice when it matters most. A king who forgets the decrees of justice is a king who forgets the oppressed. This is not abstract morality. It is the recognition that power without restraint becomes cruelty. The mother's teaching moves immediately from the king's personal discipline to his public obligation: his strength must be spent defending those with no voice, judging righteously, and standing for the poor. She teaches her son that kingship exists not to gratify himself but to protect the vulnerable. The measure of his reign will be whether he remembers the oppressed when he holds the power to forget them.

Then Lemuel's mother unveils a portrait of a woman whose excellence illuminates the very virtues her son must embody as a ruler. The woman of noble character works with purpose, builds wealth, provides for her household, and reaches out to the poor. But notice what makes her precious—more precious than rubies. It is not her efficiency or her success, though she possesses both. It is that she fears the Lord. Everything else flows from that root. She rises in darkness to labor, her hands are strong, her mind is shrewd in assessing land and vineyards, yet her strength is clothed in honor, not ambition. She laughs at the future not because she is secure in her possessions but because she knows she rests in God's hands. When snow comes and her household is clothed, the text makes clear that this provision comes from her faithful stewardship. This is what a life looks like when fear of the Lord orders every decision.

The closing verses strip away any lingering confusion about what Scripture values. Charm deceives. Beauty vanishes. But a woman who fears the Lord—that is worth praising. Let your works speak at the gates. Let your character be known. The mother has now taught her son both what to be and what to look for—a pattern that challenges every reader. If you hold power, are you using it to defend the defenseless or to feed your appetites? If you build a life, are you building it on fear of God, or on appearance, comfort, and what the world calls success? The woman in this portrait did not become excellent through one noble gesture. She became excellent through ten thousand small obediences, rising in the dark, working with her hands, remembering the poor, fearing God. She teaches all of us that true strength is not what we achieve, but whom we serve, and whether we use whatever strength we have been given to protect those who have none.
